ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken dish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed rice-and-fried-chicken preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Thai crispy chicken rice; A0CKP (chicken dish, cooked) is the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai crispy chicken rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried crispy chicken pieces served alongside a mound of steamed white rice with condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Crispy Chicken Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g nutrient fields. Values are accepted as sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken with rice (USDA ID 21420) as an analogous composite; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crispy chicken rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021, entry for white rice, boiled). The crispy batter coating contributes refined starch but represents a minority of total carbohydrate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 52.9) / 100 ≈ 38.1 for a 350 g plate; however, per-serving GL is reported for a standard 265 g rice-dominant portion (carbohydrate ≈ 40.1 g): GL = (72 × 26.5) / 100 ≈ 19.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g from fish sauce and seasoning, with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100 g) and fibre (1.0 g/100 g) offset; deep-frying adds saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced fish sauce and sweet chilli sauce, and substitute grilled chicken for the deep-fried preparation to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72) and the crispy batter coating adds refined carbohydrate, elevating glycaemic load despite moderate per-100 g carbohydrate (15.1 g); sugar is low at 1.56 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute brown rice, avoid or minimise Thai sweet chilli sauce due to added sugar, and consider grilled chicken to reduce refined carbohydrate from the batter coating.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying introduces saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g estimated) and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); sodium at 324 mg/100 g contributes to blood pressure load; cholesterol is modest at 13 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should choose grilled or baked chicken over the deep-fried preparation, limit portion size to control sodium and saturated fat intake, and avoid high-sodium condiments such as fish sauce and sweet chilli sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; nightshade-derived chilli in the sauce may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request grilled or baked chicken; Thai sweet chilli sauce should be used minimally or avoided due to added sugar and nightshade content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Crispy Chicken Rice is a popular Thai-influenced rice dish consisting of deep-fried, golden-battered chicken pieces served alongside steamed white jasmine rice, typically accompanied by Thai sweet chilli sauce and garnished with fresh coriander. The chicken is marinated in fish sauce, garlic, and aromatics before being coated in a light batter and deep-fried to achieve a crunchy exterior while retaining a juicy interior. The dish is a staple at Thai-style hawker stalls and casual dining outlets across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324 mg reflecting the fish sauce and seasoning base. The white rice component carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making glycaemic load management important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which is a clinical concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is borderline elevated, posing a moderate-to-high concern for hypertension. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to the batter coating, and contains fish allergens from fish sauce.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 as stated in the input record provenance. Saturated fat is estimated from a USDA SR Legacy analogous composite and is clearly documented. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (white jasmine rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2021, which is an approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry for fried chicken with rice (closest composite available); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Thai crispy chicken rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle per Atkinson et al. 2021 (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 265 g rice-dominant portion (carbohydrate ~26.5 g): GL = (72 × 26.5) / 100 ≈ 19.1. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical hawker preparation; individual vendor practices should be verified. 6. The dish is identified as a Thai cuisine dish; mandatory Thai-script local name (ข้าวไก่ทอดกรอบ) and romanized form (Khao Kai Thod Krob) are provided per G6 multilingual compliance rules.
